description: 10 pre-1940 public land historical sites suitable for metal detecting

locations.name: Battle of Mine Creek State Historic Site
locations.description: Site of the second largest cavalry battle of the Civil War, fought October 25, 1864. Union forces routed Confederate General Sterling Price's army, capturing two generals. Kansas state historic site with 600 acres of open battlefield accessible to the public.
locations.notes: Mine Creek Battlefield State Historic Site - publicly owned, open grounds, near Pleasanton, KS

locations.name: Coronado Heights County Park
locations.description: Sandstone hill in the Smoky Hills of McPherson County believed to be near the northernmost point reached by Francisco Vasquez de Coronado during his 1541 expedition searching for the fabled city of Quivira. The site commands panoramic views of the surrounding grasslands and was used as a lookout and campsite by Spanish explorers, later by Wichita and Pawnee people, and by Santa Fe Trail travelers. The park is owned and maintained by the City of Lindsborg in McPherson County, freely accessible.
locations.source: City of Lindsborg, McPherson County - Public Land
locations.notes: Coronado Heights County Park - publicly owned county/city park, open access, near Lindsborg, KS - metal detecting allowed in open park areas

locations.name: Kanopolis Lake Army Corps Historic Campsite
locations.description: Army Corps of Engineers shoreline land along Kanopolis Lake (Smoky Hill River) in Ellsworth County, Kansas. The Smoky Hill River corridor was a major travel and trading route for Cheyenne, Arapaho, and early Euro-American traders and settlers from the 1800s onward. Horsethief Canyon and adjacent BLM bluffs feature historic Native American pictograph panels and documented camp sites. Army Corps of Engineers and BLM managed public land with open shoreline and trail access.
locations.source: US Army Corps of Engineers / BLM Kansas - Public Land
locations.notes: Kanopolis Lake shoreline - Army Corps of Engineers public land, open access, near Kanopolis, KS - detecting allowed on Corps shoreline areas

locations.name: Hollenberg Pony Express Station State Historic Site
locations.description: Built in 1857-1858 by Gerat Hollenberg, this is the only unaltered, original Pony Express station still standing on its original site. Served the Pony Express route April 1860 to October 1861. Kansas state historic site with open grounds and visitor access.
locations.notes: Hollenberg Station State Historic Site - Kansas SHPO, public access, near Hanover, KS

locations.name: Pawnee Rock State Historic Site
locations.description: A prominent sandstone outcrop on the Santa Fe Trail used as a landmark by traders, explorers, and military expeditions from the 1820s through the 1870s. Kit Carson, Zebulon Pike, and thousands of westward migrants carved their names here. Kansas state historic site, open to the public.
locations.notes: Pawnee Rock State Historic Site - publicly owned, open grounds, near Great Bend, KS

locations.name: Fort Hays State Historic Site
locations.description: Established in 1865 to protect workers building the Kansas Pacific Railroad and to maintain order on the plains. Buffalo Bill Cody served as a scout here. George Custer commanded troops from this post. Kansas state historic site with original buildings and open grounds.
locations.notes: Fort Hays State Historic Site - Kansas SHPO, public access, in Hays, KS

locations.name: Marais des Cygnes Massacre State Historic Site
locations.description: Site of the May 19, 1858 massacre where pro-slavery raiders under Charles Hamilton killed five Free State settlers and wounded five others during the Bleeding Kansas conflict. Commemorated by John Greenleaf Whittier in verse. Kansas state historic site, open to the public.
locations.notes: Marais des Cygnes Massacre State Historic Site - publicly owned, near Trading Post, KS

locations.name: Black Jack Battlefield and Nature Park
locations.description: Site of the Battle of Black Jack on June 2, 1856, the first pitched battle between pro-slavery and Free State forces during Bleeding Kansas. John Brown led the Free State forces to victory. Now a county-owned public park and nature preserve with open grounds.
locations.source: Douglas County / Black Jack Battlefield Trust - Public Land
locations.notes: Black Jack Battlefield and Nature Park - publicly accessible, near Baldwin City, KS

locations.name: Kaw Mission State Historic Site
locations.description: Methodist mission established in 1851 to educate Kaw (Kansa) Nation children, located on the Santa Fe Trail in Council Grove. The building served as a school, church, and community center for the growing trail town. Kansas state historic site with open grounds on the National Historic Landmark trail corridor.
locations.notes: Kaw Mission State Historic Site - publicly owned, Council Grove, KS - Santa Fe Trail NHT corridor

locations.name: Alcove Spring Historic Site
locations.description: Natural spring and campsite along the Oregon Trail used extensively by emigrant wagon trains from 1841 onward. The Donner-Reed Party camped here in May 1846, where Edwin Bryant named the spring. Petroglyphs and carved pioneer names remain. Kansas state historic site and public land.
locations.notes: Alcove Spring Historic Site - publicly owned, near Blue Rapids, KS - Oregon Trail corridor
